hey @Mark Saroufim  following up on  @jdbartlett96  point — I'm Danishlynx on the GEMM leaderboard. I'm in the same boat: my kernels do real computation with sync after every call, but I suspect the benchmark events aren't capturing the full execution time due to how the kernel is compiled and dispatched. Not intentional, just a side effect of the toolchain I'm using Just want to know how you'd like us to handle this so I can stay on the right side of the rules.

Hey @Mark Saroufim 

Following up , I've now measured my actual kernel compute times with explicit device sync and wall-clock timing. The real numbers are slightly higher than what the benchmark reports. I'm working on optimizing my submissions based on honest compute times and will update my leaderboard entries accordingly. Thanks for the transparency on how you'd like this handled.

Hey all, following up on @jdbartlett96's point about the ~4μs numbers (this is NOT about caching or precomputing results). I spent some time investigating this on my end and here's what I found:

The issue: If your kernel is compiled and launched through a different HIP context than PyTorch's (e.g. via tinygrad's comgr compiler, or any hipModuleLoadData/hipModuleLaunchKernel path), the benchmark's cuda events won't capture your actual kernel execution time. You'll see ~4μs regardless of what your kernel actually computes  that's just the Python dispatch overhead.

How to check if you're affected: Add wait=True to your tinygrad HIPProgram launch, or wrap your kernel call with hipEventRecord on the SAME queue your kernel runs on. If the real time is 100x+ higher than what the benchmark reports, you're hitting this artifact.

Who's NOT affected: If you're using aiter's built-in kernels (like mla_decode_stage1_asm_fwd, fused_moe, gemm_a4w4_asm) or Triton kernels, these all launch on PyTorch's queue and the benchmark events capture them correctly.
Just wanted to share since I know a few of us were confused by the numbers. Working on honest submissions now on amd-mxfp4-mm
super thanks for these insights 🙏

@Mark Saroufim  @Sinatras I'm only able to find kernel trace for only one benchmark or one PID. All other PIDs kernel traces are missing. Can you please help if this is a known issue or am I doing something wrong?

Hi @Mark Saroufim , @daniel huang ,

(From a search in this channel, I don't think this was reported, but sorry if it has.)
I believe there is a similar issue to the one that was fixed at https://github.com/gpu-mode/reference-kernels/pull/120 in the MoE problem.
But the issue is that the bad quantization conversions are happening inside `fused_moe` from AITER. At https://github.com/ROCm/aiter/blob/31586c5f4c72e98d569c36aee7dc054ee7155e82/aiter/fused_moe.py#L1049 or other places, it seems to use the hip quantization kernels that I suspect are wrong ( https://github.com/ROCm/aiter/blob/31586c5f4c72e98d569c36aee7dc054ee7155e82/aiter/fused_moe.py#L14 ).

It can be seen by running the reference kernel with just a quantization/dequantization done to the hidden state before calling `fused_moe`: with the "correct" quantization approach from https://github.com/gpu-mode/reference-kernels/pull/120 it fails, but with the "wrong" quantization function from before (`triton_quant`), it passes, which shows that internally `fused_moe` calls the wrong quantization methods.

Attached a slightly modified reference with wrong quantization/dequantization that is passing the tests but most likely wrong.
Attached as well a slightly modified reference with quantization/dequantization that is failing the tests but should be working. 

Why is it a problem:
1) probably the AMD developer would want to fix `fused_moe`
2) for this competition it means that we have to implement wrong quantization methods and can't use the correct hardware conversion instructions (`v_cvt_scalef32_pk_fp4_f32` also mentioned in https://github.com/ROCm/aiter/issues/974 ).
3) and for anyone working on the amd-mxfp4-mm problem as well, it means we can't reuse code as in one place we have to implement the correct quantization, but not in the other

I am also attaching code for what I think could be a replacement reference using the right quantization functions and AITER APIs to do the MoE stage 1 and 2.

content:
Hi @Mark Saroufim  @daniel huang ,
I've also noticed the quantization issues raised by @Kpyy on my end, though I haven't done nearly as thorough an analysis — thanks for the detailed writeup!

I wanted to raise a related concern: since commit a846c7e, the tolerances for this problem were tightened to rtol=2e-2, atol=2e-2. The issue is that the MoE pipeline goes through gemm1 → quant → gemm2, and small differences in computation order during the gemm stages get amplified after quantization. My custom HIP kernel occasionally exceeds the tolerance by a tiny margin on certain seeds (0.02105 vs the 0.02 threshold), purely due to differences in reduction/accumulation order.

With the current tolerance, it seems like custom kernels that doesn't exactly replicate AITER's internal computation order would be somehow hard to pass. Would it be possible to relax the tolerance slightly to allow for legitimate floating-point ordering differences?
I don't have access to AMD GPUs, I can't test if that works, unfortunately :{.

content: Hey @anandasai_a  just a heads up, your GEMM score of 4.327μs looks like it might be a cross-queue timing artifact. I had the exact same issue earlier (4.33μs) where kernels launched on a separate HIP context don't get captured by PyTorch's CUDA events. If you're using tinygrad, ctypes hipModuleLoad, or any non-PyTorch kernel launch path, the timing won't be honest. You can verify by adding hipEvent with wait=True around your kernel if real compute is 100x+ slower than reported, it's the artifact. Just wanted to give you a heads up before the organizers flag it.
Thanks for looking out! I've verified my timing. The kernel does complete synchronously before the function returns, so the events capture it correctly.
